name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
حمٓ (١)
```

```english
Ha, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
```

```english
By the Manifest Book! (2)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنذِرِينَ (٣)
```

```english
We sent it down on a blessed night, and We have been warning [mankind]. (3)
```

```arabic
فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ (٤)
```

```english
Every definitive matter is resolved on it, (4)
```

```arabic
أَمْرًا مِّنْ عِندِنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (٥)
```

```english
as an ordinance from Us. We have been sending [apostles] (5)
```

```arabic
رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦)
```

```english
as a mercy from your Lord—indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing— (6)
```

```arabic
رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (٧)
```

```english
the Lord of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, should you have conviction. (7)
```

```arabic
لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
```

```english
There is no god except Him: He gives life and brings death, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers. (8)
```

 thoughtful
## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is summarized in warning those who doubt the Book regarding the punishment of this world and the punishment of the Hereafter. This is presented by explaining that it is a manifest Book revealed from Allah to the one He sent to the people to warn them. It was revealed as a mercy from Him, the Exalted, to His servants in the best of descents during the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), in which every precise matter is made distinct.

However, the people—namely the disbelievers—doubted it, playing about in their whims. The painful punishment of this world shall soon envelop them, then they shall return to their Lord, and He will take vengeance upon them after the final judgment with an eternal punishment.

Then, as a parallel to the first of the two threats, He mentions the story of the sending of Musa (عليه السلام) to the people of Pharaoh to deliver the Children of Israel, and their denial of him, and their subsequent drowning as an exemplary punishment from Him.

Next, He mentions their denial of the second threat, which is the return to Allah on the Day of Distinction (يوم الفصل). He establishes the proof that it is coming without doubt, then mentions some of its reports and what will occur therein to the guilty (المجرمين) and the various types of punishment that will afflict them, as well as the pleasant life and honorable station with which the God-conscious (المتقون) — *those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the innate protective shield of the soul against sin and divine displeasure* — will be rewarded.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: **حم وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ** **Ha, Meem. By the Manifest Book!** The *waw* is for an oath, and what is meant by the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) is the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ** **Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night, and indeed We have been warning [mankind].** What is meant by the "blessed night" (ليلة مباركة) in which the Quran was revealed is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) — *the night of decree and majesty in which the affairs of the year are determined* — as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ** **Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment** (97:1). Its being "blessed" (مباركة) refers to its being a vessel for the abundant good that spreads over creation from the vast mercy, as the Exalted has said: **وَما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** **And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment? The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months** (97:3).

The literal sense of the wording is that it is one of the nights that rotate upon the earth. The literal sense of His saying: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ** **In it is made distinct...**, which indicates continuity, is that it recurs. The literal sense of His saying, the Exalted: **شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ** **The month of Ramaḍān is one in which the Quran was sent down** (2:185), is that it recurs with the recurrence of the month of Ramadan. Thus, it recurs with the recurrence of the lunar years and occurs once in every lunar year during the month of Ramadan. As for which specific night it is, there is no indication of that in the speech of the Exalted; however, the narrations (الروايات) will be presented to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions."

What is meant by the revelation of the Book in a blessed night—according to the literal sense of His saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ** and His saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ** (97:1) and His saying: **شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْهُدى وَالْفُرْقانِ** **The month of Ramaḍān is one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance to mankind, with manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion** (2:185)—is that what was revealed was the entire Quran.

This is not contradicted by His saying: **وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً** **As a Quran We have divided it into parts that you may read it to the people leisurely, and We have sent it down piecemeal** (17:106), nor by His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً** **The faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ So it is, that We may strengthen your heart with it, and We have recited it in a measured tone** (25:32), both of which literally suggest its gradual revelation (النزول التدريجي). This is further supported by other verses such as His saying: **فَإِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُحْكَمَةٌ** **But when a definitive surah is sent down...** (47:20), and His saying: **وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ** **Whenever a surah is sent down, they look at one another...** (9:127), and others. It is also supported by countless reports containing the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

This is because it can be interpreted as the Quran being revealed twice: once collectively and as a whole (جملة) in a single night of the nights of the month of Ramadan, and once gradually and in stages (نجوما) over the period of twenty-three years, which was the duration of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) mission.

However, that which should not be doubted is that this Quran, composed of Surahs and verses with its various contexts (السياقات) corresponding to specific and personal occasions of revelation, does not admit of being revealed all at once. For the verses revealed regarding personal events and particular incidents (الحوادث الجزئية) linked to specific times, places, persons, and circumstances are only true upon the realization of their various occasions, which are scattered in time and space. If they were gathered in one time and place, they would deviate from those occasions and become something else. Thus, it is impossible to conceive of the revelation of the Quran, in its exact form and state, once as a whole and once in stages.

Therefore, if it is said to have been revealed twice, it is necessary to distinguish between the two revelations by way of brevity (الإجمال) and detail (التفصيل). Thus, it would have been revealed once in a concise, non-manifest state and once in a detailed, manifest state. By this brevity and detail, we mean what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** **[This is] a Book whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated from the presence of One who is All-wise, All-aware** (11:1), and His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** **We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise** (43:3-4). The discussion regarding the meaning of "making definitive" (الإحكام) and "elaboration" (التفصيل) has already passed in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Hud and Surah al-Zukhruf.

It has been said: What is meant by the revelation of the Book in a blessed night is the commencement of its gradual revelation on the Night of Ordainment in the month of Ramadan. Thus, the first of the verses of the Quran to be revealed—which is Surah al-Alaq or Surah al-Hamd—was revealed on the Night of Ordainment.

This view is based on a perceived contradiction between the revelation of the entire Book in one night and its gradual revelation indicated by the previous verses. You have already seen that there is no contradiction between the verses. Furthermore, as you are aware, this is contrary to the literal sense of the verses.

It has also been said: It was first revealed as a whole to the lowest heaven (السماء الدنيا) on the Night of Ordainment, then it was revealed from the lowest heaven to the earth gradually over twenty-three years, the duration of the Prophetic mission. This view is taken from the reports narrated regarding the commentary of the verses that literally suggest its revelation as a whole, and they will be presented to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

His saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ** **...and indeed We have been warning [mankind]** stands in the position of a causal explanation (التعليل). It indicates the continuity of warning from Him, the Exalted, prior to this warning. This shows that the revelation of the Quran from Him, the Exalted, is not an unprecedented thing (بدع); rather, it is a warning, and warning is an established sunnah (سنة جارية) of His that has always been carried out among the predecessors through the path of revelation (الوحي) to the prophets and messengers and their being sent to warn the people.

His saying, the Exalted: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ** **In it is made distinct every precise matter.** The pronoun in "in it" (فِيها) refers to the night. "Distinguishing" (الفرق) is the separation of one thing from another such that they become distinct (يتمايزان), and its opposite is "making definitive" (الإحكام). Thus, a "precise matter" (الأمر الحكيم) is that whose parts are not distinguished from one another, nor are its specificities and states determined, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** **There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure** (15:21).

Thus, for all matters, according to the Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي), there are two stages: the stage of brevity and ambiguity (الإجمال والإبهام) and the stage of detail (التفصيل). The Night of Ordainment—as indicated by His saying: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ**—is a night in which matters emerge from the stage of being definitive (الإحكام) to the stage of distinction (الفرق) and detail. The Quran was revealed in it, and it is one of the definitive matters that was made distinct on the Night of Ordainment.

Perhaps Allah, the Glorified, informed His Prophet of the details of the events that would occur during the time of his mission and what would coincide with the revelation of each verse, verses, or Surah of His Book, thereby necessitating their revelation. He informed him of what would be revealed of it, so the Quran was revealed to him all at once and as a whole before its gradual and fragmented revelation.

The outcome of this view is the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) awareness of the Quran at the stage of its descent to the detailed decree (القضاء التفصيلي) before its descent to the earth and its establishment in the stage of external reality (العين). According to this view, there is no need to distinguish the two revelations by brevity and detail as mentioned in the first view.

The literal sense of the speech of some is that what is meant by His saying: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ** is the detailing of the matters explained in the Quran, such as divine gnosis (معارف), laws (أحكام), and so on. This is refuted by the fact that the literal sense of His saying: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ** indicates continuity (الاستمرار). That which continues in this night through its recurrence is the detailing of cosmic matters (الأمور الكونية) after their being definitive. As for the divine gnosis and laws, there is no continuity in their detailing; if their distinction were intended, it would have been more appropriate to say: "in it was made distinct" (فيها فرق).

It has been said: What is meant by the matter being "precise" (حكيم) is its being made definitive after the distinction, not the definitiveness that precedes the detailing. The meaning would be: every definitive matter that does not change by increase, decrease, or otherwise is decreed in the night. However, the most manifest meaning is what we have previously presented.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ** **...as a command from Us. Indeed We have been sending [messengers].** What is meant by "command" (الأمر) here is "affair" (الشأن), and it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the previous "matter" (الأمر). The meaning is: in it every matter is made distinct while being an affair from Us and originating from Our presence. It is also possible that what is meant by it is the opposite of "prohibition," meaning: every matter is made distinct by a command from Us. In any case, it is connected to His saying: **يُفْرَقُ** (is made distinct).

It is also possible that it is connected to His saying: **أَنْزَلْناهُ** (We sent it down), meaning: while the Book is a command or by a command from Us. His saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ** is not without support for this, serving as a causal explanation for it. The meaning would be: We sent it down as a command from Us because Our established sunnah is the sending of prophets and messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: **رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** **...as a mercy from your Lord. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.** That is, its revelation is a mercy from your Lord, or We revealed it for the sake of bestowing mercy upon the people, or the mercy of your Lord necessitated its revelation. Thus, His saying: "mercy" (رَحْمَةً) is a circumstantial qualifier according to the first meaning, and an object of purpose (مفعول له) according to the second and third.

In His saying: **مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (from your Lord), there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural to the third person. The reason for this is to manifest the care for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), because he is the one to whom the Quran was revealed and he is the warner sent to the people.

His saying: **إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** means: the One who hears the requests and knows the needs. Thus, He hears their request and knows their need for being guided by the guidance of your Lord, so He reveals the Book and sends the Messenger as a mercy from Him to them.

His saying, the Exalted: **رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ** **the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, should you have certainty.** Since the idolaters (الوثنية) believed that every category of creation had a god or more, and sometimes a group among them took a god other than what others took, He followed His saying: **مِنْ رَبِّكَ** with His saying: **رَبِّ السَّماواتِ** etc., so that none of them would imagine that His Lordship (ربوبيته) over the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a specialized one like that which exists among them. Rather, He, the Exalted, is his Lord and the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. For this reason, He also followed it in the next verse with His saying: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** **There is no god but He.**

His saying: **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ** (should you have certainty)—this condition, as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari, is like our saying: "This is the favor of Zayd, whose generosity people have heard of and whose liberality is famous, if his report has reached you and you have been told his story." Thus, the meaning is: He is the one whom those who possess certainty (الموقنون) recognize as the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them; if you are among them, you would recognize Him as the Lord of everything.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** **There is no god but He: He gives life and brings death, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers.** Since the implication of the previous verse was the restriction of Lordship (الربوبية)—which is ownership and governance (الملك والتدبير)—to Him, the Exalted, and since Divinity (الألوهية)—which is being the rightful object of worship—is among the necessary concomitants of Lordship, He followed it with the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد) which negates every god besides Him, the Exalted.

His saying: **يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ** (He gives life and brings death) are among the most specific attributes belonging to Him, the Exalted, and they are among the affairs of governance. In mentioning them, there is a kind of preparation for what is to come regarding the warning of the Return (المعاد).

His saying: **رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** (your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers) contains a complete explicit declaration that He is their Lord and the Lord of their fathers; so let them worship Him and not make excuses by following their fathers in the worship of idols. To complete the explicit declaration, the sentence was presented in the second-person address, so it was said: "your Lord and the Lord of your fathers" (رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ).

These two—namely His saying: **يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ** and His saying: **رَبُّكُمْ**—are predicates (خبران) for a suppressed subject, the estimation being: "He (هو) gives life and brings death..." etc.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ**: "The blessed night is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (عليهما السلام)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from 'Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from 'Umar ibn Udhaynah, from al-Fudayl, Zurarah, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Humran, that he asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ**. He said: **نعم ليلة القدر وهي في كل سنة في شهر رمضان ـ في العشر الأواخر ـ فلم ينزل القرآن إلا في ليلة القدر ـ قال الله تعالى : « فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ » قال : يقدر في ليلة القدر كل شيء ـ يكون في تلك السنة إلى مثلها من قابل : خير وشر وطاعة ومعصية ومولود وأجل ورزق ـ فما قدر في تلك السنة وقضي فهو المحتوم ـ ولله تعالى فيه المشية.** "Yes, it is the Night of Ordainment, and it is in every year in the month of Ramadan—in the last ten nights. The Quran was not revealed except on the Night of Ordainment. Allah, the Exalted, said: 'In it is made distinct every precise matter.' He said: 'Everything that will be in that year until the same time in the following year is ordained on the Night of Ordainment: good and evil, obedience and disobedience, births, appointed terms (آجال), and sustenance (رزق). So whatever is ordained in that year and decreed is the definitive (المحتوم), and for Allah, the Exalted, therein is the Will (المشية).'"

I say: His saying: "it is the definitive, and for Allah therein is the Will" means that it is definitive from the perspective of causes and conditions, so nothing prevents its realization unless Allah so wills.

In *al-Basa'ir*, from 'Abbas ibn Ma'ruf, from Sa'dan ibn Muslim, from 'Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I asked him about the middle of Sha'ban, and he said: **ما عندي فيه شيء ـ ولكن إذا كانت ليلة تسع عشرة من شهر رمضان ـ قسم فيها الأرزاق وكتب فيها الآجال ـ وخرج فيها صكاك الحاج واطلع الله إلى عباده ـ فغفر الله لهم إلا شارب خمر مسكر. فإذا كانت ليلة ثلاث وعشرين فيها يفرق كل أمر حكيم ـ ثم ينهى ذلك ويمضي ذلك.** 'I have nothing regarding it. However, when it is the nineteenth night of the month of Ramadan, the sustenances are apportioned, the appointed terms are written, the documents of the pilgrims are issued, and Allah looks upon His servants and forgives them, except for the drinker of intoxicating wine. Then, when it is the twenty-third night, in it every precise matter is made distinct; then it is concluded and executed.'" I asked: "To whom?" He said: **إلى صاحبكم ولو لا ذلك لم يعلم.** "To your companion [the Imam]; were it not for that, he would not know."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Muhammad ibn Nasr, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn 'Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ**. He said: **يكتب من أم الكتاب في ليلة القدر ما يكون في السنة ـ من رزق أو موت أو حياة أو مطر ـ حتى يكتب الحاج : يحج فلان ويحج فلان.** "There is written from the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) on the Night of Ordainment whatever will be in the year—of sustenance, death, life, or rain—even the pilgrims are written: so-and-so will perform the Hajj, and so-and-so will perform the Hajj."

I say: The reports regarding the Night of Ordainment, what is decreed therein, and its identification are very numerous, and the most important of them will be presented in the commentary of Surah al-Qadr, Allah, the Exalted, willing.
